claims.text: John Titor (spelled 'Teeter' by the auto-transcript) was a self-described temporal soldier from 2036 who first contacted the public in July 1998 via faxes to Art Bell's Coast to Coast AM, then in November 2000 began posting on the Time Travel Institute message board claiming he had been sent back in time on a mission to retrieve an IBM 5100 computer.

evidence.quote:
on July 29 1998 Art Bell host of Coast
to Coast AM was running what he called
open timelines he wanted calls from
p

a little over two years after the time
traveler sent faxes to Art Bell a user
who called himself time traveler zero
and later John Teeter created an account
on a message board called the time
travel Institute on November 2nd 2000

claims.rationale: The historical existence of the Titor posts is well-documented: Art Bell did host Coast to Coast AM and ran open lines for time-traveler claimants; the Time Travel Institute forum posts dated November 2000 onward are real archived artifacts. The authorship is contested (see later claims) but the posts themselves and their content are real. The 'real time-traveler from 2036' framing is the host neutrally describing what Titor said, not asserting it is true.

claims.text: Titor's stated motive for retrieving an IBM 5100 — that it was needed to debug legacy code threatened by the 2038 Unix epochalypse — is technically coherent because the 5100 secretly retained the ability to run APL programs from older IBM mainframes, a feature IBM never advertised publicly.

evidence.quote:
Y2K did cause some
sporadic problems something similar is
going to to happen in 2038 and it could
be worse Unix systems measure time in
seconds

most
personal computers like the IBM 5100
could only support the basic programming
language but the 5100 had special
functionality that was hidden from the
public it could run programs written in
basic but it could also run APL programs
written for IBM mainframes

claims.rationale: The Unix Y2038 problem (signed 32-bit time_t overflow on 19 Jan 2038) is real and documented. The IBM 5100's hidden APL/BASIC microcode capability is also documented — it was an emulator mode IBM did not publicize. This is one of the technically strongest pieces of the Titor narrative and is the main reason it has fooled people for 25+ years.

claims.text: Titor's mechanism for time travel — rotating-singularity 'tipler sinusoid' devices invented at CERN in 2034 and built by General Electric — is dressed up in real physics terminology (Frank Tipler's 1974 cylinder paper, Kerr black holes, the Everett-Wheeler many-worlds interpretation) but no such device or research program exists.

evidence.quote:
time travel was invented in 2034
offshoots of certain successful Fusion
reactor research allowed scientists at
CERN to produce the world's first
contained Singularity engine

tipler
sinusoid or a tipler cylinder is a
theoretical method of time travel
outlined in a paper by Frank tipler in
1974

claims.rationale: Frank Tipler's 1974 paper on causality violation in massive rotating cylinders is real; Kerr black holes and Everett-Wheeler many-worlds are real physics. But CERN does not have any singularity-engine program, GE does not build time machines, and 'closed timelike curves' remain a theoretical curiosity that requires unphysical conditions (infinite cylinders or exotic matter). The framing is sci-fi window-dressing on real terminology.

claims.text: Titor's predicted near-future timeline — a U.S. civil war beginning in 2004-2005, a brief but devastating World War III in 2015 with Russia and China killing roughly three billion people, and the U.S. fragmenting into five regions with the federal capital relocated to Omaha — has comprehensively failed to occur.

evidence.quote:
the Civil War in the United
States starts in 2004 and the war
affects everyone at the age of 13 John
joins a shotgun infantry unit called the
Fighting Diamondbacks and the war
continues until 2015 and finally ends
with what he calls a very short World
War III

predicted a Civil War in the United
States that would begin in 2005.

claims.rationale: Empirically and uncontroversially false: there was no U.S. civil war in 2004-2005, no World War III in 2015, no Russian/Chinese nuclear strike on U.S. cities, and no relocation of the U.S. capital to Omaha. The host himself acknowledges all of this. The only ambiguity is the apologist 'many-worlds dodge' that any failed prediction came true on a different timeline, which is unfalsifiable.

claims.text: When confronted with these prediction failures, believers retreat to the many-worlds defense — Titor came from a different 'world line' so divergence between his timeline and ours explains every miss — which makes the entire claim unfalsifiable and therefore evidentially worthless.

evidence.quote:
predictions like that could be wrong
because he was from a different timeline
which he called A World line

True
Believers and there are still a lot of
those always fall back on the many
worlds theory

claims.rationale: This is a textbook example of an unfalsifiable rescue: any wrong prediction is shielded by 'different timeline'. The host's framing is correct that this defense renders the original claim non-empirical. Marked 'opinion' because the meta-claim about epistemics is interpretive, not factual, but the underlying logic is widely accepted in philosophy of science (Popper-style demarcation).

dependencies.rationale: The unfalsifiability charge bites only because the timeline predictions (c_0004) failed.

claims.text: A handful of Titor's more diffuse predictions — a mad-cow disease outbreak in the early 2000s, the internet evolving toward something like the cell-phone system, a decentralized video-creator industry rivaling television, and a Higgs-boson-style breakthrough at CERN — were in fact correct, though all of them are arguably the kind of educated guess a tech-literate hoaxer in 2000 could have made.

evidence.quote:
there was a
mad cow disease outbreak in the early
2000s to America's First confirmed case
of bovine spongif

the Higgs
boson was confirmed Stephen Hawking
originally thought that everything that
enters a black hole disappears forever
Pete

claims.rationale: These individual hits are real: BSE was confirmed in U.S. cattle in 2003-2004; the smartphone-mediated mobile internet came to dominate by the late 2000s; YouTube/streaming did decentralize video; the Higgs was confirmed at CERN in July 2012; Hawking did publicly walk back the strict information-loss view in the mid-2000s. directTruth around 0.65 because the individual facts are true but the host's framing slightly inflates how impressive the predictions are — they are mostly extrapolations a tech-aware writer could make.

claims.text: The leading attribution candidate for Titor is Joseph Matheny, a writer and trans-media artist behind 'Ong's Hat' — one of the earliest alternate-reality games — who has openly claimed he and three undisclosed collaborators built the Titor story as an internet-myth experiment, and whose tech and theoretical-physics background match the technical depth of the posts.

evidence.quote:
Joseph Matheny he's described
as a writer and trans media artist and
he's mostly known for aung's hat

ong's hat had references
to theoretical physics

claims.rationale: Joseph Matheny is real and is well-known in ARG history for Ong's Hat (one of the earliest networked-fiction projects, tracing back to the early-90s BBS scene). He has indeed publicly claimed credit for Titor in interviews. Whether the claim itself is true is unproven — he refuses to name collaborators or provide artifacts — so directTruth ~0.5: he is a plausible author but the attribution is not established.

claims.text: An alternative attribution, championed by independent researcher 'Hoax Hunter' John Rasmus Houston, identifies cybersecurity professional Maury Haber as the likely Titor author, with his brother Larry Haber — a Florida entertainment attorney who registered the John Titor Foundation LLC in 2003 and the John Titor trademark in 2007 — providing the legal scaffolding to monetize the story.

evidence.quote:
convinced that
John Teeter is actually Maury Haber

in 2003 he registered the John Teeter
Foundation LLC a for-profit business and
this LLC published John teeter's posts
as a book called A Time Traveler's

claims.rationale: The Larry Haber commercial-trademark facts are documented in public USPTO and Florida state filings — the John Titor Foundation LLC and trademark are real. Maury Haber's authorship is a strong circumstantial inference (text comparisons, P.O. box overlaps) but not confirmed by Maury himself. directTruth slightly above 0.5 because the commercial half is verified and the authorial half is the most-evidenced suspect after Matheny.

claims.text: Larry Haber's dual posture — claiming to represent the Titor family as their attorney while simultaneously running a for-profit LLC that publishes Titor's posts as a book and holds the Titor trademark — is a textbook conflict of interest that strongly suggests the family is fictional and Haber is in business with himself.

evidence.quote:
isn't that a conflict of interest
I think it is but I guess Larry Haber
and the Teeter family are okay with it

claims.rationale: The structural conflict — same person serving as fiduciary to the supposed real family and as commercial owner of their identity — is a fair professional-ethics critique. It does not prove the family is fictional, but combined with the absence of any public-facing 'Titor family' member, the inference that Haber is shielding either a hoax or a contrived claim is reasonable. Marked opinion because it is a judgment, not a falsifiable fact.

dependencies.rationale: The commercial LLC and trademark are themselves the conflict; Haber's role is the bridge between the legal facts and the inference.

claims.text: Titor promised believer Pamela that he would send a 'departure video' authenticated by a secret song known only to the two of them, but more than two decades later no such video has surfaced and the song has never been revealed — a textbook missing-payoff pattern for a hoax.

evidence.quote:
John
provided Pamela with a secret song to
act as a password this is something only
the two of them know so she could
identify imposters but to this day the
departure video has not arrived and
Pamela has never revealed the secret
song

claims.rationale: The non-arrival of the departure video and continuing non-disclosure of the secret song are factually accurate as of 2026 — neither has surfaced in 25+ years of intense fan scrutiny. The 'this fits a hoax pattern' framing is reasonable: a real time-traveler's mailed-back artifact would be the strongest single evidence piece, and its persistent absence is telling.

claims.text: Titor's farewell sermon — that contemporary Americans are 'lazy, self-centered, civically ignorant sheep' watching their constitution be torn away — is moralizing political commentary dressed up as a future-historian's lament, structurally identical to the way other political-fiction narrators (e.g., John Connor in the Terminator films) use the future to scold the present.

evidence.quote:
no one likes you in the future this time
period is looked at as being full of
lazy self-centered civically ignorant
sheep

claims.rationale: The Titor narrative explicitly recycles John Connor / Sarah Connor tonal beats — Matheny himself states this was intentional in the host's account of his interviews. Reading the screed as didactic political ventriloquism rather than a sincere future-warning is a defensible literary judgment.

claims.text: AJ's verdict: while he cannot conclusively determine whether Titor is real, he treats the prepper checklist Titor left behind (food/water/firearms/first-aid/Constitution-reading/community of trusted neighbors) as good general advice and is taking it for his own family — a weak Pascal's-wager framing where compliance is cheap so the EV favors hedging.

evidence.quote:
do not eat or use products
from any animal that is fed and needs
parts of its own dead

be skeptical
of John Teeter at your own risk me
I'm not willing to take the chance
thank you so much for hanging out with
me today my name is AJ

claims.rationale: The advice (food prep, first aid, community) is genuinely sensible regardless of Titor's veracity, so the Pascal-wager framing is not unreasonable. directTruth at 0.5 because the host is self-aware that this is a credulity-flavored hedge rather than an empirical endorsement; he is asserting a personal disposition, not a falsifiable claim about the world.

dependencies.rationale: The verdict pivots back to the Titor framing introduced in the opening claim.
